questions.question: Why is understanding OCD themes helpful for people with OCD?

options.text: It helps them diagnose themselves without a professional
It helps with recognition of OCD patterns and reduces shame by showing they are not alone
It allows them to avoid all their triggers permanently
It proves that their intrusive thoughts are true

questions.explanation: Understanding OCD themes serves two key purposes: it helps you recognize OCD patterns you might not have identified, and it reduces shame by showing that millions of people experience the same categories of intrusive thoughts. This recognition and normalization can also help prepare for treatment.

questions.question: What makes Harm OCD different from actual violent intent?

options.text: People with Harm OCD only think about harming strangers
People with Harm OCD are distressed by the thoughts because they clash with their values
Harm OCD only involves visible compulsions like avoiding knives
Harm OCD goes away on its own without treatment

questions.explanation: People with Harm OCD experience extreme distress precisely because the intrusive thoughts clash with their values. Research confirms they are no more likely to commit violence than anyone else. Their distress and avoidance reflect strong moral values and a deep fear of causing harm, not a desire to do so.

questions.question: Why is taboo-thought OCD the most underreported form of OCD?

options.text: Because it is extremely rare and affects very few people
Because it only affects adults, not children
Because the intense shame it causes prevents people from disclosing their symptoms
Because therapists refuse to treat this type of OCD

questions.explanation: Taboo-thought OCD creates a vicious shame cycle: the disturbing intrusive thoughts cause intense shame, which prevents people from telling anyone (including therapists), and without help, the OCD strengthens. Breaking this cycle starts with understanding that these are symptoms, not character flaws, and that OCD therapists hear these themes every day.

questions.question: What is Relationship OCD (ROCD)?

options.text: A condition where people are afraid to enter any relationships
Normal doubts that everyone has about their partner
Obsessive doubt about your relationship with constant analyzing and comparing as compulsions
A relationship problem caused by poor communication

questions.explanation: Relationship OCD (ROCD) creates obsessive doubt about relationships — 'Do I really love them?' 'What if they're not the right person?' The constant analyzing and comparing is a compulsion, not evidence that something is wrong with the relationship. It follows the same OCD cycle as other themes.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the commonly recognized OCD themes?

options.text: Contamination OCD
Symmetry and 'just right' OCD
Attention deficit OCD
Scrupulosity (religious OCD)

questions.explanation: The commonly recognized OCD themes include contamination, harm, symmetry/'just right,' taboo/unacceptable thoughts (including sexual, violent, and religious/scrupulosity themes), as well as less common themes like existential, somatic, and sensorimotor OCD. 'Attention deficit OCD' is not a recognized OCD theme.
